coredata.dc:title: Exfoliation-induced exposure of active sites for g-C3N4/N-doped carbon dots heterojunction to improve hydrogen evolution activity
coredata.dc:creator: Jiao, Yingying
Li, Yike
Wang, Jianshe
He, Zhanhang
Li, Zhongjun
coredata.dc:description:
Photocatalysis is a surface catalytic process in which photogenerated electrons are transferred to the surface of the catalyst for water reduction. In this paper, we described a novel two-step calcination strategy that not only realizes the integrating of N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with g-C3N4 nanosheets, but also can the g-C3N4/NCDs heterojunction provide more catalytic active sites and shortens the distance of charges transport to the surface of materials. The first thermal treatment in the air lead to the formation of bulk g-C3N4 while NCDs was loaded in it. The subsequent secondary calcination was conducive to the exfoliation of bulk g-C3N4 into thinner nanosheets with thickness of 1 nm. Meanwhile, more NCDs insetted in the pristine g-C3N4 were exposed to the surface of materials and more catalytic active site were formed, thus promoting the hydrogen evolution. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) tests certified that the g-C3N4 nanosheet/NCDs composites can generate more O
H radical compared with bulk g-C3N4 and bulk g-C3N4/NCDs, which further validated that photogenerated electron-hole was effective separated due to more exposure of NCDs and shorter distance of electron transport to the surface. Impressively, results showed that modification of g-C3N4 with NCDs (1.0 wt % loading) exhibited the highest photocatalytic hydrogen production rate (3319.3 μmol g−1 h−1) and an apparent quantum yield of 29.8 % at 420 nm, which was 13 folds of bulk g-C3N4. Our work illuminates a new method for high activity g-C3N4/carbon dots photocatalyst in the field of photocatalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nitride
N-doped carbon dots
Two-step calcination
Active site exposure
Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21671176 and 21001096 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of the g-C3N4 nanosheets/NCDs.
XRD patterns of BCN, CNN, CNN/NCDs-0.5 %, CNN/NCDs-1.0 %, CNN/NCDs-1.5 % and CNN/NCDs-2.0 %.
TEM images of CNN (a), NCDs (b), CNN/NCDs-1.0 % (c) and HRTEM image of CNN/NCDs-1.0 % (d).
Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of (a) BCN/NCDs-1.0 % and (b) CNN/NCDs-1.0 % on silicon wafer, with height profile determined along the lines shown in the insets.
XPS survey spectra of CNN/NCDs-1.0 % (a); high-resolution XPS of spectra of C 1s (b) N 1s (c) and O 1s (d) of CNN/NCDs-1.0 %.
(a) N2 adsorption desorption curve and (b) Pore size distribution curve of BCN, BNN/NCDs-1.0 % and CNN/NCDs-1.0 % complexes.
UV–vis DRS spectra of the as-prepared samples and band gap of g-C3N4.
FT-IR spectra of NCDs, CNN and CNN/NCDs-1.0 %.

(a) Comparison of photocatalytic hydrogen production rate over different photocatalyst (a. BCN, b. CNN, c. BCN/NCDs-1.0 %, d. CNN/NCDs-0.5 %, e. CNN/NCDs-1.0 %, f. CNN/NCDs-1.5 %, g. CNN/NCDs-2.0 %). (b) Photocatalytic hydrogen production over (CNN/NCDs-1.0 %) during 15 h, with evacuation every 3 h.

(a) Transient photocurrent responses of BCN, BCN/NCDs-1.0 % and CNN/NCDs-1.0 % in 0.5 M Na2SO4 under a 300 W Xenon lamp light irradiation. (b) EIS Nyquist plots in the dark. (c) The PL spectra of the as-prepared samples. (d) DMPO spin-trapping EPR spectra recorded with BCN, BCN/NCDs-1.0 % and CNN/NCDs-1.0 %.
The types of charge separation proposed for g-C3N4 nanosheets/NCDs under visible irradiation.
The pore size and specific surface area of BCN, BCN/NCDs-1.0 % and CNN/NCDs-1.0 %.

head.ce:title: Exfoliation-induced exposure of active sites for g-C3N4/N-doped carbon dots heterojunction to improve hydrogen evolution activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, P. R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this parper, a novel simple two-step calcination strategy is developed to prepare ultra-thin g-C3N4 nanosheets/N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) composite with superior photocatalytic hydrogen evolution.

Photocatalysis is a surface catalytic process in which photogenerated electrons are transferred to the surface of the catalyst for water reduction. In this paper, we described a novel two-step calcination strategy that not only realizes the integrating of N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with g-C3N4 nanosheets, but also can the g-C3N4/NCDs heterojunction provide more catalytic active sites and shortens the distance of charges transport to the surface of materials. The first thermal treatment in the air lead to the formation of bulk g-C3N4 while NCDs was loaded in it. The subsequent secondary calcination was conducive to the exfoliation of bulk g-C3N4 into thinner nanosheets with thickness of 1 nm. Meanwhile, more NCDs insetted in the pristine g-C3N4 were exposed to the surface of materials and more catalytic active site were formed, thus promoting the hydrogen evolution. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) tests certified that the g-C3N4 nanosheet/NCDs composites can generate more O
H radical compared with bulk g-C3N4 and bulk g-C3N4/NCDs, which further validated that photogenerated electron-hole was effective separated due to more exposure of NCDs and shorter distance of electron transport to the surface. Impressively, results showed that modification of g-C3N4 with NCDs (1.0 wt % loading) exhibited the highest photocatalytic hydrogen production rate (3319.3 μmol g−1 h−1) and an apparent quantum yield of 29.8 % at 420 nm, which was 13 folds of bulk g-C3N4. Our work illuminates a new method for high activity g-C3N4/carbon dots photocatalyst in the field of photocatalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: The efficient transformation of solar energy into storable and environmental hydrogen is identified as a promising strategy to meet future global energy demand [1–3]. The use of semiconductors to split water into hydrogen has been one of the most ideal approaches since the establishment of TiO2 electrode by Honda and Fujishima [4]. In recent years, to improve the solar-to-hydrogen efficiency, various photocatalysts (CdS, ZnO, C3N4 and Phosphorene, etc) have been developed. Among which graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) with a band gap of about 2.70 eV, has shown potential application as visible photocatalysts for photocatalytic water-splitting due to its visible-light response, excellent thermal and chemical stabilities [5–7]. Unfortunately, the sluggish photocarrier transfer of g-C3N4 is still the biggest obstacle for photocatalyst efficiency. To overcome the above defects of g-C3N4, lots of researches, including nonmetal doping [8], morphology controlling [9,10], metal deposition and incorporating with other semiconductors to form heterojunction [11–14] and so forth, have been performed. Among those strategies, constructing heterojunction has also been proved to be an effective strategy, since it can induce the built-in potential in the interface, thereby promoting the separation of photogenerated carriers [15–19].

Various heterojunctions have been constructed, such as ZnS/g-C3N4 [20], TiO2/g-C3N4 [21], g-C3N4-PEDOT-Pt [22], CoP/g-C3N4 [23], carbon dots/g-C3N4 [24,25]etc. Particularly, carbon dots/g-C3N4 heterojunction is extensively constructed through different synthetic strategies because of its unique light induced electron transfer [26,27]. For instance, Kang et al. reported that g-C3N4/CDs exhibited certain activity for water splitting [28]. Liu et al. reported CDs modified ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets composite, displaying high photocatalytic activity, which illustrated that this heterojunction structure could effectively improve the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance of g-C3N4 [25]. Although a few efforts have been made to integrate CDs with g-C3N4 and remarkable results have been achieved, as far as we know, it has not been reported that thermal exfoliation of g-C3N4/CDs heterojunctions to form thinner nanosheets and increase the exposure of CDs and photocatalytic active sites.

In the current work, we described a novel two-step calcination strategy that ultimately afforded N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) modified g-C3N4 nanosheets with more exposed catalytic active sites compared to conventional calcination-derived g-C3N4. X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Transition electron microscopy (TEM) revealed that the accumulation of g-C3N4 sheets was effectively inhibited and more carbon dots were exposed. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image further displays that the obtained CNN/NCDs-1.0 % has a height of ∼1 nm (3 atomic layers) (Fig. 3b and its inset), demonstrating the resultful exfoliation process for the generation of thinner g-C3N4 sheet. Electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) and transient photocurrent responses (TPR) showed that g-C3N4 nanosheets/NCDs composite can efficiently accelerate the separation and transmission of photogenerated carriers, which was further verified by photoluminescence spectra (PL) characterization. Results showed that modification of g-C3N4 with NCDs (1.0 wt % loading) exhibited the highest photocatalytic hydrogen production rate (3319.3 μmol g−1 h−1) and an apparent quantum yield of 29.8 % at 420 nm, which was 13 folds of bulk g-C3N4. These results provide a fundamental understanding of the structural characteristics of g-C3N4/NCDs for excellent photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity. In addition, we systematically study the effect of synthetic condition on the photocatalytic activity and physicochemical properties of the as-prepared g-C3N4 nanosheets/NCDs composite.

The synthesis of NCDs was based on a previous reported method [29]. Briefly, 1.0 g of urea and 3.0 g of citric acid were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water. Then the above mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-sealed autoclave and kept it at 180 °C for 6 h. The product was collected and adjusted to neutral to obtain the NCDs solution after being cooled to room temperature. The product was dried at 70 °C.

3g of dicyandiamide was mixed with 20 mL NCDs aqueous solution (contain different amounts of NCDs). Then the mixture was vaporized at 70 °C. The obtained sample was served in a covered crucible and heated at 550 °C for 3 h with a heating rate of 2.8 °C/min. The obtained sample was recorded as BCN/NCDs-x, x = 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 %, respectively, meaning different weight fraction of NCDs at 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 wt%. Bulk g-C3N4 (BCN) was synthesized in the alike process as bulk g-C3N4/NCDs except that NCDs was not added.

BCN/NCDs-x was put in a corundum crucible without a cover and calcined at 500 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 2 °C/min. The prepared sample was noted as CNN/NCDs-x. In order to study the effect of heating conditions on the photocatalytic performance of the composite, the BCN/NCDs-1.0 % were heated at different temperatures and holding times (T = 450 °C, 500 °C, and 550 °C. t = 1.5 h, 2.0 h and 2.5 h) at a ramping rate of 2 °C/min in a muffle furnace. The prepared sample was noted as S450−2h, S550−2h, S500−1.5h, and S500−2.5h, respectively. g-C3N4 nanosheets (CNN) was prepared in the same process as g-C3N4 nanosheets/NCDs except that BCN/NCDS-x was replaced with bulk g-C3N4.

Crystal structure was analyzed by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), and crystallinity of the sample by using an Empyrean Multipurpose Research Diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were obtained on a Thermo Escalab 150 spectrometer with Al Kα X-rays. The morphology of samples were recorded using a JEOL 2100 TEM microscope and SEM S3400. The fluorescence properties analysis were performed over a Hitachi fluorescence spectrometer (F-4600). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area was recorded by using an aperture meter (NOVA 1000e) and Quantachrome specific surface at 77 K. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra were measured using on a Cary 5000 UV–vis spectrometer. FTIR spectra were recorded using a Nicolet Nexus 470 spectrometer. Electrochemical measurement was performed on standard three-electrode system in which Na2SO4 (0.5 M) was used as the electrolyte, catalyst as working electrode, Ag/AgCl as reference electrode and platinum plate as counter electrode. The sample was tested by the AC Impedance mode of the RST 5200 electrochemical work station for the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. A 300 W Xe lamp with 420 nm filter was used as the light source to filter out the ultraviolet light. Amperometric i–t curve mode was used to conduct the instantaneous photocurrent test.

The Photocatalytic hydrogen activities of samples were investigated using CEL-SPH2N photolysis water production system. A 300 W Xenon arc lamp (Profect Light, PLS-SXE300C) with an optical filter (λ > 420 nm) was used as the light source. 50 mg of as-synthesized photocatalyst was suspended in 100 mL of aqueous solution (containing 10 vol.% triethanolamine as a sacrificial agent). 2.5 % Pt was photodeposited onto the samples surface by adding H2PtCl6. Before the light irradiation, the suspension system was bubbled with high-purity N2 gas for 40 min. The amount of hydrogen evolution was measured by every 1 h on GC-7920 gas chromatography.

The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) for hydrogen evolution was tested with a band-pass filter (ƛ = 420 nm). The apparent quantum efficiency was calculated according to the following formula:
A Q E =
2 × t h e number of evolved
H 2
molecules
t h e number of incident photons
× 100 %
The method for synthesizing the g-C3N4 nanosheets/NCDs complex via two-step calcination was schematically illustrated in Scheme 1 .

To reveal the crystal and chemical structures of these samples, the XRD patterns of the g-C3N4 and CNN/NCDs with diverse mass ratios of NCDs were showed in Fig. 1 . The XRD peaks could serve as (002) and (100) reflectance indices around 27.4° and 13.1°, corresponding to the interlaminar stacking of carbon nitride and the inplane structure filling motif, respectively [30]. For all CNN/NCDs composite, the (100) peak became weaker, which described the planar size of the layers decreased because of thermal exfoliation of bulk g-C3N4 [31]. The results of XRD (Fig. 1) of CNN/NCDs were not obvious different from that of bulk g-C3N4, demonstrating that the coupling of NCDs with g-C3N4 nanosheets did not alter the structure of g-C3N4. In addition, when the NCDs loading was gradually increased, the diffraction intensity of the (002) peak gradually decreased. These results indicated that the presence of NCDs could inhibit the polymerization of tri-s-triazine units and the accumulation of g-C3N4 sheets. In the case of CNN/NCDs, the (002) diffraction peak was slightly shifted to a bigger angle than BCN, which indicated the decreased interlayer distance because of the enhanced interaction among the layers [31]. In addition, in order to investigate the effect of calcination conditions on the crystal and chemical structures of the sample, the XRD patterns of S450−2.0h, S500−2.0h, S550−2.0h, S500−1.5h and S500−2.5h were showed in Figure S1. The results displayed that the intensity of the (002) peak of S500−2.0h is the weakest, which indicated that the polymerization of the tris-triazine unit and the accumulation of g-C3N4 sheets could be effectively inhibited under the condition of calcination at 500 °C for 2 h.

In order to gain deep insight into the microstructure details of the prepared samples, the morphology of the samples was recorded by HRTEM and TEM. The appearance of g-C3N4 nanosheets in Fig. 2a was very similar to the appearance of graphene, which was consistent with that reported in the literature [31]. The TEM image in Fig. 2b showed that the NCDs are uniformly distributed. It could be seen from the TEM image (Fig. 2c) that NCDs were equably loaded on the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets, declaring the existence of heterostructures between g-C3N4 and NCDs. The lattice size of NCDs is 0.26 nm(Fig. 2d), matches well with the (100) plane of graphite [32]. Compared with CNN/NCDs-1.0 %, BCN/NCDs-1.0 % material consists of dense and thick layers and unobvious NCDs (Fig S3), which indicated that the thermal oxidation etching could further exfoliate bulk g-C3N4 resulting in more exposure of NCDs. The morphology of S450−2h, S550−2h, S500−1.5h and S500−2.5h were displayed in Fig S4. The changes in calcination temperature and holding time may lead to differences in carbon nitride thickness and NCDs exposure extent, which may cause differences in photocatalytic hydrogen production performance.

Theoretically, the number of active sites of carbon dots is inversely proportional to the thickness of the lamella. The thinner the thickness is, the more exposed the active sites are. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images display that the thickness of the sample before and after secondary thermal exfoliation is approximately 20 and 1 nm, respectively (Fig. 3a-b), which demonstrating the effectiveness of secondary thermal exfoliation to further reduce the thickness. In order to explore the influence of thermal polymerization conditions on the product layer thickness, AFM test was also performed on S450−2h, S550−2h, S500−1.5h and S500−2.5h (Fig S5). The heights of S450−2h, S550−2h, S500−1.5h and S500−2.5h are ∼6.0 nm, ∼5.0 nm, ∼4.0 nm, and ∼2.0 nm, respectively. The results showed that the sample prepared at 500 °C for 2 h has the minimum layer thickness, which thus could expose more reaction sites, which are consistent with the results of photocatalytic activity.

The XPS pattern of CNN/NCDs-1.0 % was showed in Fig. 4 , only the signals of the C, N, and O were showed in the survey spectrum (Fig. 4a). The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 4b) at 284.7 eV is ascribed to the surface adventitious carbon. The major C peak at 288.2 eV was idexed as NCN bonds, which was the basic substructure unit of g-C3N4, the peak at 285.8 eV was idexed residual CO, whereas the lower one at 284.7 eV was contributed to CC bonds, that was usually displayed in the XPS spectrum of carbon nitride [33,34]. As for N1 s spectrum (Fig. 4c), it presented four characteristic peaks corresponding to CNC sp2 of tri-s-triazine rings(398.7 eV), bridged nitrogen atoms N–(C)3 groups (400.2 eV), amino functional groups (C-N-H)(401.2 eV) and π→π* satellite band in heptazine rings(404.3 eV), respectively [35–37], which was similar to the reported literature [38]. The O 1s spectrum showed two peaks at 533.9 eV and 532.1 eV, which was indexed to CO and CO, respectively.

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption test was performed on BCN, BCN/NCDs-1.0 % and CNN/NCDs-1.0 %. The results were given in Fig. 5 and Table 1 . The specific surface area of CNN/NCDs-1.0 % was obvious higher than that of BCN/NCDs-1.0 %. The results elucidated that the preparation of the material by the two-step calcination method facilitated the dissociation of g-C3N4 into layer of sheet-like structure, thereby increasing the pore and specific surface area of the material, providing more active site adsorption reactants. The CNN/NCDs-1.0 % displayed much higher specific surface area because of thermal exfoliation, promoting exposure of more active sites to H+. In order to explore the influence of heating conditions on the specific surface area of the sample, nitrogen adsorption-desorption test was also performed on S450−2h, S550−2h, S500−1.5h and S500−2.5h (Fig S6). The BET surface areas are 25.18, 153.50, 65.08, and 133.00 m2 g−1 for S450−2h, S550−2h, S500−1.5h and S500−2.5h, respectively. The results showed that the sample prepared at 500 °C for 2 h has the largest specific surface area, which could expose more reaction sites. This result was consistent with the AFM test results shown in Fig S5.

The UV–vis DRS of BCN, CNN, and CNN/NCDs was showed in Fig. 6 . Compared to BCN, the absorption edge of CNN exhibited an obvious blue shift, which was ascribed to the quantum confinement [39]. Moreover, as the content of NCDs increases, the absorption of the CNN/NCDs samples gradually increased, and the absorption edge gradually red shift, which promoted the catalyst to fully utilize visible light because of the enlarged light absorption region, thereby enhancing photocatalytic activity. According to Kubelka-Munk, the bandgap of g-C3N4 nanosheets and bulk g-C3N4 are 2.76 eV and 2.60 eV, respectively (Fig. 6b). The band-edge position of the composite was important for the transfer of photoexcited charge carriers. The valence band and conduction band position of g-C3N4 could be evaluated by Eqs. (1) and (2) [40].
(1) EVB = χ ‒ Ee + 0.5 Eg
(2) ECB = EVB − Eg

In this work, χ means the electronegativity of g-C3N4. Its value is 4.72 eV [41], and the Ee value is 4.5 eV (vs NHE). According to the above formula, the valence band positions and conduction band positions of g-C3N4 nanosheets are 1.60 eV and −1.16 eV, respectively. As shown in Fig S7 (a) and Fig S7 (b), valence band positions and conduction band positions of NCDs were 0.57 eV and 1.87 eV, respectively.

The chemical bonds of NCDs, CNN and CNN/NCDs-1.0 % were investigated by FT-IR spectra (Fig. 7 ). CNN and CNN/NCDs-1.0 % showed an obvious absorption peak at 812 cm−1, which corresponded to the tris-triazine unit [42]. The peaks were located at 1242, 1319, 1461, 1575 and 1635 cm−1, attributing to N-(C)3 and CNHC unit, which demonstrated the formation of net-work of CNC bonds [43]. Absorbance in the range from 3650 to 3000 cm−1 is associated with the residual NH groups and the OH band.

The results of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate of as-obtained samples were presented in Fig. 8 a. Pure g-C3N4 showed a poor photocatalytic activity due to the high recombination of photogenerated carriers. Nevertheless, when NCDs was loaded onto g-C3N4, the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity was obviously improved, which demonstrated the incorporation of NCDs with g-C3N4 have a positive effect on the photocatalytic H2 activity. When NCDs loading was increased from 0.5 % to 1.0 %, the hydrogen production rate of the photocatalysis was increased. The composites CNN/NCDs-1.0 % showed the highest photocatalytic hydrogen activity. The hydrogen production rate could reach to 3319.3 μmol g−1 h−1, which was 13 and 4.7 folds of BCN and CNN, respectively. It was worth noted that the efficiency of the CNN/NCDs-1.0 % was higher than previously reported carbon dots/g-C3N4 photocatalysts (Table S1). Further loading of NCDs onto g-C3N4 resulted in a decrease in the photocatalytic hydrogen production performence. This phenomenon may be attributed to the hiding effect, which lead in the decrease of the absorption of light. Moreover, in order to further study the effects of calcination temperature and calcination time on the hydrogen production performance of the catalyst, we investigated the hydrogen production performance of the samples prepared at different calcination conditions (Fig S8). The results showed that the photocatalyst obtained by calcination at 500 °C for 2 h displayed the best hydrogen production performance, which may be attributed to its large specific surface area and the exposure of more NCDs.

In order to explore the stability of the photocatalyst, the time-circle of H2 evolution over CNN/NCDs-1.0 % had been carried out (Fig. 8b). After five uninterrupted cycling photocatalytic experiments, the H2 evolution rate did not display obvious decrease (3128.6 μmol g−1 h−1) (Fig S11), indicating the outstanding stability of the CNN/NCDs-1.0 % composite. In addition, the crystal structures of CNN/NCDs-1.0 % composites were not changed as evidenced by the XRD (Fig. S2) and SEM (Fig S10), which indicated that the photocatalyst are superior stable under consecutive visible light irradiation.

Transient photoelectrochemical response measurements were investigated by the electrochemical experiments (Fig. 9 a). It was clear that the photocurrent density for CNN/NCDs-1.0 % photocatalytic was much larger than that of BCN. This demonstrated that the photogenerated electron-hole separation effect of CNN/NCDs-1.0 % was the most obvious, which was consistent with the PL test results shown in Fig. 9c [44,45]. EIS Nyquist plots were constructed to study the advantage of CNN/NCDs-1.0 % composite over BCN in the transport processes of photogenerated carriers. As shown in Fig. 9b, a smaller arc had been presented for CNN/NCDs-1.0 % composite as compared to that of BCN, which further indicated smaller charge transfer resistance of electron–hole pairs in the CNN/NCDs-1.0 % composite. The semicircle in the Nyquist plot can be simulated well by an electrical equivalent-circuit model (Fig. 9b inset). The results shown that CNN/NCDs-1.0 % (Rp: 31069 Ω) has a much smaller semicircle radius than BCN (Rp: 48376 Ω) and BCN/NCDs-1.0 % (Rp: 34169 Ω), which indicates more efficient interfacial charge-carrier transfer in CNN/NCDs-1.0 %. This result was accordant with the trend of the photocurrent response, which illustrated that the binding of NCDs to g-C3N4 nanosheets could significantly promote photoinduced charge transfer and separation, thereby effectively enhancing photocatalytic hydrogen evolution.

To explain the influence of NCDs on the photocatalytic hrdrogen activity of g-C3N4, photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra was performed to study the recombination rate of photogenerated carries (Fig. 9c). The PL emission intensity of CNN/NCDs-1.0 % was dramatically decreased compared to BCN and BCN/NCDs-1.0 %, which demonstrated that photogeerated carriers were most efficiently separated in CNN/NCDs-1.0 %. Moreover, photoluminescence (PL) analysis test was also performed on S450−2h, S500−2h, S550−2h, S500−1.5h and S500−2.5h (Fig S9). The results showed that the photocatalyst obtained by calcination at 500 °C for 2 h displayed the weakest PL emission intensity, which is match well with the hydrogen production performance of the CNN/NCDs composites. The intensity of EPR of CNN/NCDs-1.0 % is stronger than that of BCN and BCN/NCDs-1.0 %, illustrating the efficient generation of photogenerated carrier pairs.

In order to further reveal the oxidation and reduction reactions occurring on the surface of the photocatalyst, the energy band arrangement of the composite was determined by combining the XPS spectrum and the band gap energy (hν) of NCDs (Fig. S7). Based on the previous work of our group, the electronic band structures of the composite were schematically showed in Fig. 10 and its inset. By aligning the experimental results with the established band alignment, the direction of the photogenerated charge on the heterojunction of the composite material could be inferred. The photogenerated electron–hole pairs were excited to CB, when both g-C3N4 and NCDs were irradiated by visible-light. The electrons from CB of NCDs would pass on to VB of of g-C3N4 nanosheets and combine rapidly with the holes. The stronger reductive electrons on the CB of g-C3N4 were used to participate in hydrogen evolution process. The disadvantage of fast carrier recombination in the g-C3N4 photocatalyst was effectively suppressed by constructing the 0D/2D Z-scheme heterostructure. This mechanism explained the roles of NCDs in the g-C3N4 nanosheets/NCDs composite and why the g-C3N4 nanosheets/NCDs exhibited much higher activity for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution than that of g-C3N4.

In summary, we described a novel two-step calcination strategy that ultimately afforded N-doped carbon dots(NCDs) decorated g-C3N4 nanosheets with more exposed catalytic active sites compared to conventional calcination-derived g-C3N4. With the change of calcination conditions, the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-holes gradually changes. On the contrary, parameters of secondary calcination has influence on specific surface area and thickness of the layer of g-C3N4, as reflected by the results of AFM, BET surface areas, and TEM. Under the visible light irradiation, the CNN/NCDs-1.0 % could significantly split water with H2 evolution rate up to 3319.3 μmol g−1 h−1, which was 13, and 4.9 folds of BCN and CNN, respectively. CNN/NCDs-1.0 % composites exhibited excellent stability for long term irradiation. More importantly, our work might supply a simple and versatile method to construct novel carbon dots material combined with g-C3N4 for enhancing its photocatalytic activity.

Yingying Jiao: Conceptualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Yike Li: Methodology, Validation, Conceptualization, Investigation. Jianshe Wang: Writing - review & editing. Zhanhang He: Writing - review & editing. Zhongjun Li: Supervision, Project administration, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos.21671176 and 21001096).
